abstract:
High-valent iron-oxo species are essential intermediates in enzymatic C–H bond activation, yet replicating their high-spin ($S = 2$) configuration and reactivity in synthetic platforms remains a major challenge. The instability of terminal iron-oxo moieties toward dimerization and ligand oxidation often hampers their performance in molecular systems. This study demonstrates that the metal-organic framework $\text{Fe}_2\text{(dobdc)}$ and its magnesium-diluted variant, $\text{Fe}_{0.1}\text{Mg}_{1.9}\text{(dobdc)}$, can overcome these hurdles by providing site-isolated iron centers within a rigid, weak-field ligand environment.

Using nitrous oxide as a terminal oxidant, we show that these frameworks can successfully convert ethane into ethanol and acetaldehyde under mild conditions. Characterization via powder neutron diffraction and infrared spectroscopy reveals that $\text{N}_2\text{O}$ binds weakly to the iron(II) sites in an unusual bent geometry. Upon activation, the system likely generates a transient iron(IV)-oxo intermediate before relaxing into a stable iron(III)-hydroxide species, $\text{Fe}_2\text{(OH)}_2\text{(dobdc)}$. Electronic structure calculations, including DFT and CASPT2 methods, support the existence of a high-spin $S = 2$ ground state for the active iron(IV)-oxo species. These findings highlight the capability of metal-organic frameworks to stabilize rare electronic configurations and facilitate the functionalization of strong C–H bonds.
